title: Dice Order

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: Three standard six-sided dice are rolled in SEQUENCE. We observe the values rolled in order.
rules: Each die has values {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} with equal probability.
We want the rolled values, IN ORDER, to be strictly increasing (e.g., 2, 4, 6 -- but NOT 2, 2, 6 or 6, 4, 2).
setup.question: What is the probability the three rolls form a strictly increasing sequence?

learningObjectives: Decompose: P(strict increase) = P(all distinct) * P(strict increase | all distinct).
Compute P(all distinct) via the standard 'pick distinct' calculation.
Apply symmetry: 3 distinct values arrange into 3! orderings, only 1 is strictly increasing.

stages.label: Decompose into two factors
stages.prompt: Strict increase requires (1) all three values to be DISTINCT, and (2) the order to be the increasing one. Decompose the probability.

options.text: P(strict increase) = P(all distinct) * P(strict order | all distinct).

stages.explanation: Multiplication rule: condition on 'all distinct' first, then on the ordering being increasing GIVEN distinct.

stages.label: Compute P(all distinct)
stages.explanation: 1 * (5/6) * (4/6) = 20/36 = 5/9 ≈ 0.5556. (1st die is anything; 2nd avoids 1st; 3rd avoids both.)

stages.label: Compute P(strict | all distinct)
stages.prompt: Given 3 distinct values, how many orderings are there, and how many are strictly increasing?

options.text: 3! = 6 orderings, 1 is strictly increasing.  So P = 1/6.

stages.explanation: Three distinct values can be arranged in 3! = 6 ways; only one (the sorted ascending one) is strictly increasing.

stages.label: Commit
stages.explanation: 5/9 * 1/6 = 5/54 ≈ 0.0926. About 9.3% of all 3-dice rolls are strictly increasing.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What's P(non-strict increase, i.e., non-decreasing)? (Hint: includes ties.)
What's P(strict increase) for 4 dice rolls?
Compare to asking: 'roll 3 dice and SORT them ascending.' Why is that always 'strictly increasing' (when distinct)?
